title: How Do Financial Constraints Affect Product Pricing? Evidence from Weather and Life Insurance Premiums
abstract: I identify the effects of financial constraints on firms' product pricing decisions, using insurance groups containing both life and property & casualty (P&C) divisions. Following P&C divisions' losses, life divisions change prices in a manner that can generate more immediate financial resources: premiums fall (rise) for life policies that immediately increase (decrease) insurers' financial resources. Premiums change more in groups that are more constrained. Life divisions increase transfers to P&C divisions, suggesting P&C divisions' shocks are transmitted to life divisions. Results hold when instrumenting for P&C divisions' losses with exposure to unusual weather damages, implying that the effects are causal.

article.introduction:
How frictions in financial marketsaffect firms' real activities is a fundamentally important question in finance. These frictions can cause firms to be financially constrained. I study how financial constraints affect firms' product pricing decisions, which are decisions that all firms face and have significant implications for customers. Studying this question is important for understanding how financial shocks transmit to the real economy. Such an investigation can also shed light on one of the channels through which a systemic financial shock, such as the 2008 to 2009 financial crisis, can result in product price movements.
The prior literature on how financial constraints affect product pricing faces two challenges. First, it is difficult to identify causal effects of financial constraints on pricing decisions, as both can be affected by other forces such as suppliers' pricing decisions. Second, the prior literature presents seemingly contradictory results: Some papers find that when more constrained, firms raise prices, while other papers find firms lower prices. This paper contributes to the literature by confronting the identification challenge and showing a plausible causal link between financial constraints and product pricing. I further show that when facing more constraints, firms change product prices in different directions for different products, and thereby highlight a mechanism that can reconcile contrasting results in prior literature.
To identify causal effects of financial constraints on product pricing, I use the insurance industry as a laboratory, which allows me to exploit plausibly exogenous shocks to life insurers' financial constraints. Some insurance groups contain both life and property & casualty (P&C) divisions. Losses to P&C divisions, such as those caused by severe weather events, can worsen P&C divisions' financial conditions. As a result, life divisions may increase financial transfers to P&C divisions (observable in the data), which is one way P&C divisions' losses can tighten life divisions' financial constraints. Such shocks to P&C divisions are unlikely to directly affect life divisions' business through other channels.
To estimate how life divisions change pricing decisions following losses to P&C divisions of the same insurance group, I construct a sample of life insurance premium quotes at the insurer-month-product level from 216 life insurers from 2002 to 2013. The empirical analyses capture price changes relative to the same product offered by other insurers in the same month by controlling for product-month fixed effects. The price changes I capture are also relative to the same product offered by the same insurer in other months, as I also control for insurer-product fixed effects. Around half of the life insurers belong to groups that also contain P&C divisions. I refer to a conglomerate containing multiple insurance divisions as a group, and I refer to both standalone insurance companies and insurance divisions that belong to groups as insurers. If two insurers belong to the same group, they are regarded as affiliated.
I find that, following P&C divisions' losses, life divisions of the same group change prices in different directions for different policies. First, life divisions' premiums for a class of permanent life policies decrease with P&C divisions' losses. Under these permanent policies, customers pay fixed annual premiums until the insureds die and the insurer pays the death benefits. The average annual premiums in this market are high relative to the costs in the early years after issuance, so insurers immediately gain financial resources (cash flows, assets, and capital) when selling such policies. By lowering premiums, life divisions can sell more of these policies and bring in more immediate financial resources.
Second, by contrast, life divisions' premiums for 10-year term policies increase with P&C divisions' losses. Under such policies, customers also pay fixed annual premiums. The insurer pays death benefits if the insured dies within the 10-year policy duration. The market-average annual premiums for these policies are low relative to insurers' costs in the early years, so insurers initially incur an outflow of financial resources when selling such policies. By raising premiums, insurers reduce the outflow for each policy and sell fewer such policies, reducing total immediate outflow.
The finding that prices change in two opposite directions helps alleviate endogeneity concerns: It is unlikely that an omitted variable would be related to larger P&C losses, as well as lower premiums for the permanent policies and higher premiums for the 10-year policies. Moreover, reported P&C losses contain measurement error and may not reflect true losses, as studies document that P&C insurers sometimes overstate or understate incurred losses. To mitigate endogeneity concerns and the measurement error issue, I construct an instrument for P&C losses using P&C divisions' lagged market share in each state, as well as state-quarter-level unusual weather damages relative to the state's historical averages of the same quarter. The results on how life divisions change premiums in response to P&C losses hold.
I next find that life divisions change premiums to a greater extent when they belong to insurance groups that are ex ante more financially constrained. Intuitively, for such groups, a given P&C loss is likely to have a larger impact on the entire group's financial constraints and as a result lead to larger responses in life divisions' pricing decisions.
Finally, I find that when P&C losses are larger, life divisions make more financial transfers to the rest of the group. We would expect such transfers if insurers optimally allocate resources by equalizing the shadow value of financial resources across divisions following adverse shocks to P&C divisions. The final two results corroborate the argument that life divisions change prices because P&C losses worsen life divisions' financial constraints.
Despite the specifics of the insurance setting, the intuition applies to noninsurance firms that face frictions in the external capital markets. Assuming that financial constraints are more severe when firms have low levels of cash or high levels of debt, if a division suffers an adverse financial shock that does not affect its investment opportunities, the affected division should receive more internal capital allocation. The other divisions may change their product pricing in a way that generates more near-term cash flows, in particular, lowering prices for products with immediate payoffs and raising prices for products that require initial outlays.
This paper contributes to several strands of the literature. First, it contributes to the literature on how firms' financial constraints affect product pricing, in two ways. One way is by using plausibly exogenous financial shocks to firms to improve identification and thereby establish a causal effect of financial constraints on product pricing decisions. Another way is by highlighting that whether firms raise or lower product prices can depend on products' short-term effects on firms' financial conditions. This mechanism is likely to operate in nonfinancial industries as well and could explain why some papers find that firms raise prices, while other papers find that firms lower prices when they become more constrained.1
The permanent life policies, those that generate immediate financial resources for insurers at issuance, are analogous to products for which customers pay before firms incur marginal production costs and to inventories that have already been produced. By lowering prices, firms could sell more in a shorter time period and gain more immediate liquidity, consistent with findings based on the trucking industry by Zingales (1998), and the airline industry by Borenstein and Rose (1995) and Busse (2002).
The 10-year term policies, those that initially drain insurers' financial resources, are analogous to products for which customers pay after firms incur marginal production costs. Firms' limited liquidity reduces their production capacity, for example, by limiting the raw material needed for production that they can afford. With product market frictions, firms can raise prices to clear a smaller supply, consistent with findings based on the grocery industry by Chevalier (1995a) and Chevalier and Scharfstein (1996), the discounter industry by Khanna and Tice (2005), the manufacturing industry by Campello (2003), and products underlying the U.S. Producer Price Index by Gilchrist et al. (2017).2
In addition to highlighting a mechanism that can reconcile the different findings across industries, this paper indicates that within a given industry, firms may change prices in different directions depending on the timing of incurring costs and collecting payoffs, for example, when they pay suppliers and when they receive revenues. Hence, movements of price indices aggregated across firms or industries can mask rich heterogeneity in price increases and decreases. Using aggregated prices can therefore lead to an incomplete understanding of the relationship between financial constraints and product pricing.
Second, my results relate to the literature on financial intermediation. On the one hand, insurers selling permanent policies, which generate immediate financial resources, is similar to banks attracting deposits. I find that when they are more constrained, insurers lower prices on these policies. This result is analogous to stressed banks offering more attractive deposit rates as documented by, for example, Acharya and Mora (2015), Egan, Hortaçsu, and Matvos (2017), and Ben-David, Palvia, and Spatt (2017). On the other hand, selling 10-year policies is similar to banks making loans, as both have an initial negative impact on intermediary capital. Insurers raising prices on these policies mirror stressed banks raising loan spreads as shown by, for example, Adrian and Song (2008), Benetton (2020), and Buchak et al. (2020).
Despite these similarities to the banking literature, this paper makes several distinct contributions. As insurers are significant intermediaries with $9.9 trillion of liabilities (see Federal Reserve Financial Accounts of the United States Z1, 2019Q4), understanding their behavior and its impact on other agents in the economy—especially when insurers' financial health weakens—is important to understanding all channels of shock transmission via the financial sector. Moreover, life policies insure against mortality risks that are more diversifiable than the aggregate risks that banks are exposed to through deposits and loans. Thus, from the banking literature, one may not necessarily infer my results: Prices for households to insure mostly idiosyncratic risks can also depend on intermediaries' financial health.
Third, this paper contributes to the literature on internal capital markets. Prior studies (e.g., Lamont (1997), Shin and Stulz (1998), Fee, Hadlock, and Pierce (2009), Matvos and Seru (2014), Seru (2014), Giroud and Mueller (2015), Giroud and Mueller (2017)) focus on how internal capital markets allocate limited internal resources to be spent on investment, R&D, and employment.3In contrast, I study product pricing, which is directly involved in generating financial resources. My findings differ by implying that when some divisions' financial constraints tighten, other divisions change behavior in a way consistent with generating and securing more immediate financial resources. In addition, this literature derives most of its evidence from public firms. In contrast, around 80% of the firms in my sample are private and arguably more constrained than public firms. In this regard, I contribute to the literature by offering evidence based on private firms.
Finally, although this paper aims to shed light on the general question of how financial conditions affect pricing decisions, it also contributes to our understanding of how financial frictions affect life insurers' pricing, which is an important topic as more than 71% of U.S. households own life policies.4Koijen and Yogo (2015) find that life insurers lowered markups for permanent life policies and annuities during the financial crisis. Koijen and Yogo (2021) show that fees on variable annuities increased during the financial crisis. Sen and Humphry (2018) find that U.K. insurers raised prices of capital-intensive pension and annuity contracts after changes in capital regulations. By using idiosyncratic and plausibly exogenous shocks to life insurers, I can more cleanly separate the effect of financial constraints from the effects of forces (e.g., competition and demand) due to systemic shocks used by the three papers above. Furthermore, this paper provides evidence on the link between two insurance markets that are largely considered independent of each other. Although I exploit idiosyncratic shocks in the analysis, my results imply that a systemic shock to either the life or the P&C market can impact customers in the other market.
The paper proceeds as follows. SectionIsummarizes the institutional background and develops the hypotheses. SectionIIdescribes the data. SectionIIIpresents results on the effect of P&C divisions' losses on life divisions' pricing decisions. SectionIVpresents results on how P&C divisions' losses affect internal capital transfers. Finally, SectionVconcludes.
article.I. Institutional Background and Hypothes:
This paper is intended to have general implications for how firms' financial conditions affect product pricing beyond the insurance industry. As I argue above, the insights here can be generalized across industries. However, to explain how life insurers can relax their financial constraints by changing policy premiums, I begin by discussing the specific forms of financial resources relevant to insurers.
Like noninsurance firms, insurers can face financial constraints due to capital market frictions such as moral hazard or asymmetric information. Also like other firms, insurers can be constrained by liquidity. Insurers can further be constrained by assets and capital (assets relative to liabilities), for three reasons.5First, some insurance products require initial outlays in terms of cash and capital. Such products can be seen as insurers' investment opportunities. Second, to monitor insurers, regulators analyze insurers' financial health, including capital and cash flows.6When insurers' capital and cash flows are low, regulators are more likely to intervene today or in the future, and thus the shadow value of financial resources increases. Third, customers' demand increases with better ratings.7The leading insurance rating agency, AM Best, considers many aspects of insurers' financial conditions, including liquidity, assets, and capital (AM Best (2004), AM Best (2009)). TableIA.Iin theInternet Appendixsuggests that ratings improve with insurers' assets and capital, in a concave manner.8Therefore, demand should increase with assets and capital, and more so when assets and capital are low.
For the above reasons, the shadow value of cash, capital, and assets increases at P&C divisions following their losses, assuming frictions in external capital markets. Ge and Weisbach (2021) find that P&C insurers shift toward safer corporate bonds in their investment portfolios following losses, suggesting that these losses are meaningful shocks to insurers. I hypothesize that life divisions make transfers to P&C divisions following P&C losses. Such transfers can happen as an optimal response to maximize the value of the entire group. These transfers can also happen if the internal capital markets are “socialistic” as in Rajan, Servaes, and Zingales (2000), Scharfstein and Stein (2000), and Matvos and Seru (2014). Transfers from life to P&C divisions can increase the shadow value of financial resources at life divisions.
I next illustrate how life divisions can change policy premiums in a way that increases immediate financial resources in terms of cash flows, assets, and capital, the shadow value of which increases after losses. A new policy changes insurers' cash flows and assets in expectation both by an amount equal to premium minus expenses minus actuarial costs each year, ignoring taxes. Expenses include commissions paid to the selling agents, as well as underwriting costs due to health screening, analyzing applications, etc., so expenses are high in the first year. Thus, issuing a policy can initially raise or lower insurers' cash flows and assets depending on whether premiums exceed expenses and actuarial costs in the near term.
Selling policies also changes insurers' capital (assets minus liabilities) each year by an amount equal to premium minus expenses minus actuarial costs minus reserves. Reserves are present values of expected losses calculated according to statutory rules and are recorded under liabilities. Issuing a policy can initially increase or decrease insurers' capital depending on whether, initially, premiums exceed the sum of expenses, actuarial costs, and reserves.
The first category of policies that I study is guaranteed universal life policies with fixed annual premiums and no savings component. A policy is in effect as long as the insured is alive and the premium is paid. I refer to such policies as permanent policies since they are often called “permanent term policies.” Average annual premiums are high relative to the sum of expenses, actuarial costs, and reserves in the initial years. The example in TableI, Panel A, illustrates how such a policy initially increases insurers' capital, cash flows, and assets.
I hypothesize that life divisions' premiums for such policies fall with P&C divisions' losses. By lowering premiums, life insurers can sell more of these policies and attract more inflows of financial resources. In the later years of the policy, as the insureds become older and expected payouts become larger, the net outflow of financial resources will be larger with lower annual premiums. However, when they face more constraints, life divisions can be seen as effectively increasing the discount rate applied to the future outflows and assigning an increased relative weight to near-term financial resources versus future financial resources.
The second category of policies that I study is 10-year term policies. Policyholders pay a fixed annual premium. Average annual premiums are low relative to the sum of expenses, actuarial costs, and reserves in the initial years. The example in TableI, Panel B, illustrates how such a policy initially lowers insurers' capital, cash flows, and assets.9
I hypothesize that life divisions' premiums for such policies rise with P&C losses. By raising premiums, life divisions reduce the initial outflow for each policy sold. They will also sell fewer such policies as they raise prices, decreasing total initial outflows.
The intuition behind the two hypotheses on price changes is similar to that in Chevalier and Scharfstein (1995). Outside of insurance, product prices can also affect firms' current and future profits. For example, low prices today may reduce today's profits, but can boost future profits by locking in a sticky customer base. Thus, to maximize the present value of profits, optimal prices can be above or below the single-period profit-maximizing price. When financial constraints tighten, to increase short-term profits, firms move prices toward the single-period profit-maximizing levels by raising (cutting) prices if prices are below (above) these levels.
article.II. Data:
Data on monthly life insurance prices come fromCompuLife, which sells software that generates price quotes for insurance agents and maintains a policy premium comparison website. Life insurance premiums differ based on insureds' gender, age, health, policy length, and death benefits. The pricing data for the first category of policies I use, the permanent policies, come from Koijen and Yogo (2015), who extracted the data fromCompuLife. The data on these policies cover products with a death benefit of $250,000 for nonsmoking males and females aged 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, and 80 in the regular health class. Each combination of age, gender, health status, and death benefit amount (e.g., for a 40-year-old male in regular health with a death benefit of $250,000) defines a product, so there are 12 products. This sample contains 27,976 monthly price quotes between January 2005 and July 2011.
I extract the pricing data for the second category of policy I use, the 10-year term policies, fromCompuLife. I collect prices for nonsmoking males aged 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, and 80 in three health classes (preferred plus, preferred, and regular) with a death benefit of $250,000 or $500,000. Each combination of age, health status, and death benefit amount defines a product, so I have 36 products. This sample contains 312,310 insurer-month-product quotes between 2002 and 2013. TableAIIin AppendixAlists each product's mean and median premiums. Panel E of TableIIpresents summary statistics for premiums both in dollar terms and as a percentage of the average for the same product across my full sample.
The life insurance prices in my sample correspond to 216 life insurers, of which 103 belong to 50 groups that also have P&C divisions. There are 391 P&C divisions in the sample. Insurers' financial data come from their statutory reports, obtained from the National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) and SNL Financial. Annual financial variables are available from 1995 to 2013, with some variables available from 1991 using insurers' 1995 filings of “Five-Year Historical Data.” Quarterly variables are limited to the period 2001 to 2013. I also obtain data from AM Best's Insurance Reports, which contain insurers' financial strength ratings and other financial measures calculated by AM Best. Variables from AM Best are limited to 2003 to 2013 except for ratings. Each of AM Best's reports gives insurers' most recent ratings, which can be dated before 2003. Life insurers do not report the number of policies sold in detailed categories, which prevents tests on the implications of pricing changes on the number of policies sold.
Insurers with negative assets are excluded. P&C insurers with net premiums written less than $10,000 are also excluded since they do not have meaningful operations. If an insurer changes ownership in year, it is excluded for bothand. Financial variables, except ratings, are winsorized at the 1% and 99% levels. TableII, Panel A reports summary statistics on financial variables for life insurers in my pricing sample, Panel B for P&C divisions affiliated with these life insurers, and C for the groups that contain them.
P&C insurers report net underwriting gain, which in short is equal to premiums minus losses incurred minus expenses, net of reinsurance; TableAIinAppendix Aoffers a detailed definition. To analyze how life divisions change policy premiums in response to P&C divisions' losses, I aggregate P&C divisions at the group level. First, I sum the quarterly net underwriting gain across P&C divisions to obtain the group-level P&C underwriting gain. If the sum is negative (i.e., P&C divisions as a whole incur net losses), its absolute value represents group-level P&C losses. If the sum is positive, I set group-level P&C losses to zero. The variableis the group-level P&C losses as a percentage of the sum of previous-year-end total assets across P&C divisions.
article.III. Life Insurance Prices and Affiliate:
In SectionI.B, I hypothesize that for the permanent policies, life divisions will lower premiums following P&C divisions' losses. In contrast, for the 10-year term policies, life divisions will raise premiums. TableAIIinAppendix Aillustrates how each of the products impacts insurers' financial resources in the first two years on average. Of the 12 permanent life products, 10 (12) have a positive impact on life insurers' capital in the first year (first two years); and 11 (12) have a positive impact on life insurers' cash flows or assets in the first year (first two years). Of the 36 10-year term products, 23 (28) reduce insurers' capital in the first year (first two years), while 23 (16) reduce insurers' cash flows or assets in the first year (first two years). All but one of the 10-year products that do not cause an outflow of financial resources in the first year correspond to insureds aged 70 or older. According to an industry survey (Friedrich and Saip,2018), insureds aged 70 or older account for less than 2.2% of term policy sales. Additional evidence supports my prediction of how insurers change premiums for these two categories of policies when more constrained. During the financial crisis, markups of the permanent policies declined (Koijen and Yogo,2015), while premiums of 10-year term policies increased, which practitioners attribute to the high cost of capital.10
Insurer-product fixed effects,, control for time-invariant differences across insurers for each product.11To absorb aggregate trends over time for each product, I include year-month-product fixed effects,, where month(e.g., April 2011) falls in quarter(e.g., 2011Q2) of year(e.g., 2011).
Vectoris a set of lagged financial variables of the life insurer, which Koijen and Yogo (2015) assume affect demand elasticity and thus should affect optimal pricing. Because these variables are only available annually, I control for these variables from year, so that they are not affected by P&C losses. I also include dummy variables for insurers' ratings (e.g., A++, A+). Given ratings are updated throughout a year, I use the latest rating observable at the end of.
Because a group can have more than one life division, each of which offers multiple life products each month in a given quarter, these insurer-product-month observations shareand are subject to the same group-quarter-level shocks. Therefore, when instrumenting for, the residuals in the first-stage regressions of P&C losses should be highly correlated within a group-quarter. Moreover, policy premiums by life divisions are likely to be subject to the same group-quarter-level shocks and hence the residuals in regressions of life insurance pricing should also be highly correlated within a group-quarter. I therefore correct standard errors for clustering of observations at the group-quarter level to allow for such error correlations. In the main results, I also presentt-statistics when standard errors are corrected for clustering at the group level to allow for the possibility that life insurance pricing could be correlated within a group over time. Panel A of TableIA.VIin theInternet Appendixpresents results using six alternative clustering methods.
Although P&C and life divisions' businesses should be largely unrelated, endogeneity concerns can remain with the ordinary least squares (OLS) estimation of equation (1), as P&C divisions' reported losses are subject to not only shocks to the realized losses, but also endogenous decisions that could be directly related to life divisions' decisions as well. However, it is unlikely that an omitted variable can explain the results below: An increase in P&C losses is associated with life divisions lowering premiums on some policies while raising premiums on others. Regardless, instrumenting for P&C losses with a strong instrument that is less likely to be correlated with life divisions' pricing decisions can be reassuring.
The second concern with the OLS estimation is that reported P&C losses can be noisy measures of the true losses. P&C insurers can manipulate reported losses as documented in the literature since reported losses include estimates of losses incurred but not settled.12They sometimes understate or overstate losses. Such measurement error in reported P&C losses can attenuate the OLS results.
Moreover, setting measurement error aside, reported P&C losses can still be noisy proxies for financial constraints. For example, when future expected losses increase with realized losses, P&C divisions do not need additional capital to underwrite capital-intensive policies or to improve ratings to boost demand. They can instead seek premium increases, cut costs, and shrink business, as demonstrated by the auto insurance business between 2015 and 2017.13Such losses of P&C divisions can be negatively correlated with their need for financial resources. Hence, it is not optimal for life divisions to transfer capital to P&C divisions, and life divisions do not become more financially constrained. The OLS regression using reported P&C losses can therefore mask the true effect of financial constraints on pricing. However, after weather-related losses, investment opportunities for P&C insurers improve, as argued by Gron (1994), Froot and O'Connell (1999), and others (see Harrington, Niehaus, and Yu (2013) for a review). Additional capital becomes more valuable for P&C divisions. Thus, variation in P&C divisions' losses due to weather events could better reflect the variation in insurers' financial constraints.
For the above reasons, I instrument forusing state-quarter-level unusual weather damages, as well as P&C divisions' lagged market share in each state. I first construct unusual weather damages using the Spatial Hazard Events and Losses Database for the U.S. (SHELDUS). The data set includes every natural hazard event that caused injury, death, or property/farm damage since 1960 in the United States and provides estimated monetary damages for each event. The event types include hurricanes, wildfires, and tornadoes, among others.
First, I aggregate the dollar amount of weather damages to properties from SHELDUS at the state-quarter level to construct, wheredenotes the state andthe quarter. I obtainby subtracting the state-quarter historical average from. For each stateand quarter, I compute rolling historical averages using data on statebefore quarter(inflation-adjusted to dollars in quarter). Weather damages have seasonality. To capture the surprise component of the weather shock, the historical average for quarteruses data for the same three-month period in prior years. For example, the historical average weather damage for Florida in 2012Q3 is calculated using all historical damages in Florida in Q3 of the years from 1960 to 2011.
Figure1, Panel A displays the spatial distribution of the mean and medianwithin each state. States on the Gulf Coast, which are prone to hurricanes, rank the highest in mean but among the lowest in median. These states experience negative, meaning smaller weather damages compared to historical averages, 75.6% of the time in my sample.
Second, I construct the lagged market share of P&C divisionin stateand quarteras's direct premiums written in stateover the four preceding quarters divided by the sum of direct premiums written by all P&C insurers operating in stateduring the same four quarters. I denote the lagged market share by.
Figure1, Panel B, displays the mean and median of the state-group-year-level market share using insurance groups in my pricing sample that contain P&C divisions. These groups' P&C operations span many states, with the 25thpercentile equal to 38 and the median equal to 51 states. The highest average market share in a state is 1.2%.
Third, I multiplybyand sum the resulting product over all states for each P&C division. The sum,, approximates division's total exposure to unusual weather damages in. I sumacross all P&C divisions at the group level, and scale it by the previous year-end total assets of all P&C divisions to obtain the group-quarter-level instrument. The variable, which is aggregated over four quarters, instruments forin equation (1).
TableII, Panel C, reports summary statistics for the instrument. Figure1, Panel C, displays the mean and median of the product ofand state-group-year-level market share. TableIA.XIIin theInternet Appendixoffers summary statistics for each state.
The instrument can satisfy the relevance restriction if P&C insurers' exposure to unusual weather events is strongly related to their reported losses. To satisfy the exclusion restriction, the instrument should not be correlated with factors affecting life insurance pricing that are orthogonal to independent variables in the assumed model. In other words, the instrument should not affect life divisions' pricing decisions other than by transmitting financial shocks from P&C to life divisions. In particular, one may be concerned that the instrument reflects P&C divisions' endogenous choices on their operations and that such endogenous choices correlate with their life affiliates' pricing decisions. However, it is highly unlikely that insurers' endogenous choices on their P&C operations can cause the correlation between premiums and P&C losses to be positive for some life policies and negative for others as shown below.
Even if insurers expect some states to have larger average, and insurers have different preferences for where to operate and how to diversify their operations, all of the regressions use fixed effects to absorb time-invariant differences across insurers so that such differences do not confound the results. A number of studies in meteorology show that the distribution of hurricanes, which account for the largest share of weather damages in my data (32%), has been stationary.14Thus, it is reasonable to assume that historical weather damages represent expected weather damages, and it is hard for insurers to predict state-quarter-level, especially since the property policies in effect in quartercan be sold several quarters before. In analyses in SectionIA.Vof theInternet Appendix, I do not find support for the results being driven by losses predicted by hurricanes.
Additional results suggest that the instrument is unlikely to reflect insurers' endogenous choices, which offers further suggestive evidence in support of the untestable exclusion restriction. SectionIA.IIof theInternet Appendixexplores the source of variation in the instrument and reported P&C losses. TableAIIIinAppendix Asummarizes the results. If insurers endogenously choose their exposure to unusual weather damages, and such ability is persistent, there could be persistent across-insurer variation in the instrument. I find that the instrument does not have a persistent insurer-fixed component, while reported P&C losses do. Thus, the instrument is more likely to be exogenous to insurers' choices than reported losses, and hence the variation in the instrument is useful to recover the causal effect of financial constraints on life divisions' pricing. (Both the instrument and the reported losses have some aggregate variation over time, so I use time or time-product fixed effects to absorb it in regressions so that it does not confound the results.)
In TableIII, Panels A and B, I examine the hypothesis that, after P&C divisions suffer losses, life divisions lower premiums for policies that immediately increase insurers' financial resources, namely, the permanent policies. Intuitively, with elastic demand, lower premiums can lead to larger demand and attract more financial resources. Panel A presents the OLS results of equation (1). The coefficients onare all negative and statistically significant, suggesting that premiums for permanent policies decrease in P&C losses. The coefficients of the controls are reported in Panel C of TableIA.VIin theInternet Appendix.
Panel B presents the instrumental variable results. In the first-stage results, whereis the dependent variable, the coefficients on the instrument are all positive and statistically significant, suggesting that exposure to unusual weather damages affects reported P&C losses. An unadjusted Cragg-Donald WaldF-statistic for testing the strength of the instrument can be inflated in this setting. The reason is that in the first stage, the same values of the dependent variable and the instrument, both at the group-quarter level, are used multiple times in the estimation for all the insurers within a group. To address this concern, I take the group-quarter averages of policy premiums and each of the independent variables, so that I have only one observation per group-quarter. I repeat the regression using these group-quarter averages and controlling for group as well as quarter fixed effects to obtain an adjusted Cragg-Donald WaldF-statistic. TheF-statistic is at least 35.7, higher than the Stock and Yogo (2005) 10% threshold of 16.4 in all three specifications, suggesting that the instrument is sufficiently strong.
In the second-stage results, the coefficients on the instrumentedare all negative and statistically significant, suggesting that adverse financial shocks to P&C divisions cause life premiums to decline for the permanent policies. Based on column (6), a one-standard-deviation increase in(2.5% of lagged assets) is associated with an average decrease in annual premiums of $108, which is 1.8% of the mean ($6,130) and 14.6% of the average product-month-level standard deviation ($738).15
The results support the idea that when life divisions become more constrained after P&C losses, premiums fall for products that initially bring in financial resources. Lower premiums can attract more financial resources with elastic demand, analogous to constrained firms selling financing instruments at a discount (e.g., Lim, Schwert, and Weisbach (2019)).
I next examine the hypothesis that for policies that cause an initial outflow of financial resources, the 10-year term policies, premiums should increase with P&C losses. By raising premiums, life insurers will sell fewer such policies and reduce the outflow of financial resources. In TableIII, Panel C, I re-estimate the OLS specifications in Panel A with the sample of 10-year term policies. All of the coefficients onare positive and statistically significant, suggesting that life divisions charge higher premiums for 10-year term policies after P&C affiliates suffer larger losses.
Panel D presents results from the instrumental variable estimation. In the first-stage results, the coefficients on the instrument are all positive and statistically significant. Using the same approach above, the adjusted Cragg-Donald WaldF-statistic is at least 19.5, indicating that the instrument is sufficiently strong.
In the second-stage results, the coefficients on the instrumentedare all positive and statistically significant, suggesting that P&C losses cause prices to increase for the 10-year term policies. Based on column (6), a one-standard-deviation increase inis associated with an increase in annual premiums of $116.7, which is 4.7% of the mean ($2,468) and 24.1% of the average product-month-level standard deviation ($485).16
The results using the 10-year policies support the idea that when life divisions can become more constrained due to P&C divisions' losses, premiums increase if the products cause an initial outflow of financial resources. Higher premiums can decrease the number of policies sold and reduce the outflow of financial resources from each policy, and thus reduce the total initial outflows of financial resources caused by selling such policies. This result is analogous to firms cutting investment when constrained.
Koijen and Yogo (2015) document that the permanent policies' average markup declined from near zero before the financial crisis to −57% during the crisis. Koijen and Yogo (2021) show that fees on variable annuities increased by 17% during the financial crisis. Systemic shocks used in these papers can impact many forces likely to affect insurance pricing, such as demand, competition, and prices of alternative financial products. This paper can better separate the effect of financial constraints from other forces, and thus is better able to shed light on how idiosyncratic financial shocks affect product prices. However, the effects I document are not directly generalizable to severe systemic shocks. Instead, the effects here have clean implications for one of the channels through which systemic shocks can affect pricing in insurance and other markets.
In TableIII, the coefficients onfrom the instrumental variable estimation have larger magnitudes than those from the OLS estimation. There are two possible explanations. First, as mentioned in SectionIII.B.1, P&C insurers can manipulate reported losses, making reported losses noisy proxies for true losses. Thus, the OLS estimates of the coefficients oncan be biased toward zero, understating the true effect ofon life policy premiums.
Second, as also discussed in SectionIII.B.1, P&C divisions' reported losses can sometimes be negatively correlated with their need for financial resources. Compared to reported losses, weather-instrumented P&C losses are more likely to be associated with improved investment opportunities at the P&C divisions and tighten entire groups' financial constraints, leading life divisions to respond by changing product prices. Since reportedis a worse proxy for tightened financial constraints, its estimated coefficient in OLS will mask the true effect of financial constraints on pricing.
However, other differences between weather-related P&C losses and the reported P&C losses could also explain the difference between the OLS and instrumental variable estimates. When interpreting the instrumental variable estimates, one should exercise the usual caution about local treatment effects uncovered using the instrumental variable method.
It is useful to see if the results hold in a coarser univariate sense to verify that the results are not driven by the full model specification structure. Accordingly, I compare average life premiums between subsamples with high versus low affiliated P&C losses. Mirroring equation (1), I demean life policy premiums, as well as their P&C affiliates' reported and instrumented, by subtracting insurer-product and product-month averages. Instrumented losses are predicted losses from the first stage of the instrumental variable regression as in column (1) of Panels B and D in TableIII. I classify the demeaned(reported or instrumented) as high if it is in the top quintile and low otherwise. I only use life insurers with P&C affiliates so that the two subsamples are more comparable. Panel F of TableIIIcompares the average demeaned premiums between the high-and low-subsamples. For the permanent policies, average premiums in the high-subsample are $116.2 (using reported losses) or $107.4 (using instrumented losses) lower than in the low-subsample. For the 10-year policies, average premiums in the high-subsample are $35.7 or $33.6 higher. All four of the differences are statistically significant.17TableIA.IIIin theInternet Appendixpresents univariate mean comparisons using changes in life premiums and changes in P&C losses. The results are qualitatively similar.
Figure2presents binned scatter plots of the demeaned life insurance premiums against demeaned affiliated P&C losses. The plots show patterns consistent with the results in TableIII: As P&C losses increase, life divisions lower premiums for permanent life policies and raise premiums for 10-year term policies. If affiliated P&C losses cause life divisions to change pricing, the average price changes should be larger when P&C losses are larger. TableIA.IVin theInternet Appendixtests this hypothesis by repeating the main regressions in TableIIIafter replacingwith two dummy variables, namelyand. The results suggest that life divisions do indeed react more to larger affiliated P&C losses. I also present robustness checks in TableIA.Vin theInternet Appendix, estimating equation (1) using different subsamples of products as well as alternative dependent variables, including the natural log of premiums and premiums scaled by product averages.
Columns (1) and (2) in TableIV, Panel B, present results using the 10-year term policies. The estimatedis positive and statistically significant, which seems to suggest that life divisions raised premiums of these policies before P&C affiliates incurred losses. However, the reported losses include estimated unpaid losses. It could be the case that after suffering large losses in the periodto, insurers continue to estimate and report large losses in the periodto. This could be due to insurers smoothing income by reporting some of the actual losses incurred intoin the financial filings forto. It could also be the case that P&C insurers overstate losses intoto present large total losses overtoto gain regulators' permission to increase rates, which is required in many property insurance lines. Therefore, the noise in reported losses in periodtomay be related tointoand in turn to life divisions' pricing in quarter. To test this possibility, I instrument losses intowith unusual weather exposure over the periodto.
The results, presented in columns (3) to (5) of TableIV, Panels A and B, confirm this conjecture: when P&C divisions suffer weather shocks into, they continue to report losses into, as indicated by the first-stage results. The second-stage results suggest that losses reported intorelated to weather shocks intoare correlated with prices. These results are consistent with the argument in SectionIII.B.1that reported losses are subject to insurers' manipulation, as documented in the literature.
To see whether pretrends exist in my instrumental variable regression setting, I next examine whether life premiums in quarterare related to P&C affiliates' losses intoinstrumented by unusual weather exposure into. Columns (6) to (8) of TableIV, Panels A and B present the results. The second-stage coefficients on the instrumented future P&C losses are statistically insignificant, suggesting that no pretrends in life premiums precede the instrumented P&C losses.
Figure3plots the relationship betweenin any four consecutive quarters instrumented by unusual weather exposure in the same period and life divisions' premiums in the six months preceding the instrumented losses. The figure shows that instrumented losses are not statistically significantly related to past life premiums, indicating that life divisions' pricing does not change before P&C divisions' weather-predicted losses.18
Figure3also indicates a persistent effect of P&C losses on life divisions' premiums: The effect is statistically significant at the 10% level eight months after P&C losses for the permanent policies and 12 months after P&C losses for the 10-year policies. The average coefficient during these periods is −53.8 for the permanent policies and 43.4 for 10-year term policies. The magnitude of the effect peaks around one quarter after P&C losses, potentially due to some operational inertia in the process of updating premiums.
TableV, Panel A, presents the results with permanent policies. Columns (1) to (6) report OLS estimation results. The variable,, equals one if in year, (1) the group's assets are below the median, (2) the group's RBC ratio is below the median, (3) the group's capital-to-asset ratio is below the median, (4) the average rating of the group's insurance divisions is worse than the median, and (5) the life insurer belongs to a group in which no entity is publicly traded. A group is potentially more constrained if more of these measures equal one. I calculate the sum of these five measures of. If the sum is above the median,is equal to one in column (6). The pairwise correlation of the six dummy variables ranges between −0.5 and 0.6.
The coefficients onare all negative and are statistically significant in four of the six columns. Column (1) implies that, following a one-standard-deviation increase in, life divisions in smaller groups reduce premiums by $119.4 more than do life divisions in larger groups, which is equivalent to 16.2% of the average product-month-level standard deviation.
In column (7), I present the instrumental variable results using the composite measure of. In the first stage, I instrument forandusing the weather instrument and its interaction with. The Cragg-Donald WaldF-statistic is 10.7, above the Stock and Yogo (2005) 10% threshold of 7.0. The coefficient onin the second stage is negative but statistically insignificant.19
The estimated coefficient on,, represents the average effect ofon life premiums among less constrained insurers. I find thatis not statistically distinguishable from zero in any column, suggesting that P&C losses do not have a statistically significant average effect of life divisions' pricing among the less constrained groups. The sum ofandrepresents the effect ofin the more constrained subsample, which I report at the bottom of Panel A. The estimates of () are negative in all seven columns and statistically significant in four. The results suggest that the effects of P&C divisions' losses on life divisions' premiums are concentrated in the more constrained groups.
Panel B repeats the analysis in Panel A after adding interactions betweenand each of the control variables,, which allows the relationships between the premiums and the controls to differ between the more constrained and less constrained subsamples. The results are qualitatively similar to those in Panel A. However, the difference in the effects between the more constrained and less constrained subsamples is statistically significant only in one column, potentially because the added interaction terms decrease power. The estimates of (), which captures the effects of losses in the more constrained groups, are negative and statistically significant in six columns.
Panels C and D repeat the analyses in Panels A and B using the 10-year policies. The estimates of () are positive and statistically significant in all columns, while the estimates ofare mostly positive but statistically significant in fewer instances. These results suggest that the effects of P&C losses on 10-year policies' premiums are more prevalent in the more constrained than the less constrained groups. The coefficients onare positive and statistically significant in three (five) columns in Panel C (D). These results imply that the effects of P&C losses are larger in the more constrained than in the less constrained groups. In column (7) of Panel D, the estimate of () suggests that following a one-standard-deviation increase in, life divisions in more constrained groups increase premiums by $144.4, or 29.8% of the average product-month standard deviation. This increase is $102.8 larger than that in less constrained groups, as indicated by the estimate of.
Overall, the evidence largely supports the idea that if the groups are more financially constrained, P&C losses have a larger effect on life divisions' premiums.
When life divisions affected by affiliated P&C losses change prices, the effectiveness of the price changes in securing more immediate financial resources depends on demand elasticity. Koijen and Yogo (2016) estimate the elasticity for 10-year policies to be 2.2. Relative to 10-year policies, permanent policies have much higher annual premiums and longer policy duration. Thus, customers may shop around more, possibly leading to a higher elasticity than 2.2. Life policy premium comparison websites also help raise elasticity. On average, 214 life insurers sell ordinary policies in each of the continental U.S. states each year between 2002 and 2014. Life insurers generally sell policies in many states, with the 25thpercentile being 49 states.20Using the number of new ordinary life policies sold, an insurer's average market share is 0.5% in a state, and the average state-year-level Herfindahl index is 375.
A life insurer can use additional strategies to affect customer demand, even if elasticity could be limited due to customers' heterogeneous utility associated with a certain insurer (e.g., their familiarity with the brand). One such strategy is to increase advertising for policies that immediately bring in payoffs and simultaneously decrease advertising for policies that require initial outflows. In addition, insurers can boost demand for permanent policies by raising commission rates on these policies in addition to lowering their premiums. Similarly, insurers can further decrease sales of 10-year term policies by lowering the commission rates on these policies while increasing their premiums. Egan, Ge, and Tang (2021) find that commissions play an important role in life insurers' variable annuity sales.
With elastic demand and insurers' additional strategies to affect demand, it is plausible that the premium changes can result in meaningful increases in near-term financial resources.
How much financial resources do the documented price changes bring? Insurers do not report the number of permanent policies sold but do report the total number of term policies sold (although without a breakdown across different policy durations). InAppendix B, I use the price changes for the 10-year policies for back-of-the-envelope calculations under different assumptions. Using the median size of group-level P&C assets, a one-standard-deviation increase in P&C losses, $75.0 million, corresponds to an increase in capital of $6.0 million and an increase in cash flows (or assets) of $3.6 million in the first two years brought by premium changes. These magnitudes are averages based on the instrumental variable estimation. The magnitudes are around half as large using OLS results, which likely underestimates the effect as argued in SectionsIII.B.1andIII.C.4. I also base these estimates on the number of term policies that life divisions sell. The total changes to financial resources may be larger than presented here as life divisions can generate additional financial resources by changing prices on other products as well, including the permanent policies studied in this paper.
One of my main results is that life insurers reduce prices for permanent life policies following losses to P&C affiliates. A potential alternative explanation is that when life insurers make transfers to P&C affiliates, life insurers' financial strength weakens. Similarly, one may be concerned thatis associated with losses or lower sales for life divisions, which can weaken life divisions' financial strength directly. Because potential policyholders prefer financially stronger life insurers, the demand curve could shift downward as in Titman (1984) and Hortaçsu et al. (2013), which would lead prices of permanent policies to fall following P&C losses.
This alternative explanation is unlikely to hold for four reasons. First, prices of 10-year policies increase with affiliated P&C losses, and by more for more constrained groups. Second, the permanent policies in my sample are guaranteed by state guaranty funds.21Third, using 24 different specifications, TableIA.VIIin theInternet Appendixsuggests that life insurers' ratings on average do not deteriorate following affiliated P&C losses. Fourth, TableIA.VIIIin theInternet Appendixrepeats the main results in TablesIII, controlling for dummy variables for life insurers' ratings the month before the price quotes to account for the effect of life insurers' financial strength on their premiums. The estimated effects of affiliated P&C losses on life insurance premiums remain similar to those in the main analyses.
A second alternative explanation is that when P&C losses are large after weather events in certain regions, demand for life insurance can change in these regions. If life divisions have similar market footprints across states as their P&C affiliates, weather events related to P&C divisions' losses can change demand for life divisions, which can cause them to change life insurance premiums. However, it is unlikely that demand for permanent policies weakens and causes premiums to decrease, while demand for 10-year policies strengthens and causes premiums to increase. In addition, I find that premiums of 10-year term policies increase more for more financially constrained groups. Demand is unlikely to increase more for these insurers. Nonetheless, to further address the second alternative explanation, TableIA.IXin theInternet Appendixcontrols for life insurers' own exposure to unusual weather damages using their own lagged market share in each state. Life insurers' own exposure should reflect shocks to their customer demand due to weather events. The effects of P&C losses on life policy prices remain highly similar to those in the main results, suggesting that the price changes associated with P&C losses are not likely due to any direct impact of weather on life insurers' demand.
A third explanation is that competitors' price changes can explain the results. The regressions on life insurance prices include year-month-product fixed effects to control for aggregate trends, essentially comparing premiums against the product-month averages. To illustrate the potential concern in interpreting the results, take the 10-year term policies as an example. Competitors may have incentives to reduce prices to “prey on” insurers that suffered negative financial shocks by seizing their market share as in Chevalier (1995b). Thus, instead of life insurers with affiliated P&C losses raising premiums, the results could be due to other life insurers lowering premiums, which lowers the market-average premiums. In this case, life insurers with affiliated P&C losses appear to raise prices relative to the market average. This specific story does not explain the negative relationship between P&C losses and permanent policy premiums. Nonetheless, in SectionIA.IVof theInternet Appendix, I address this concern by constructing a proxy for market-average life insurer-affiliated P&C losses and a proxy for life insurer-specific exposure to competitors' affiliated P&C losses. The results suggest that life insurers without affiliated P&C losses do not change prices in reaction to these proxies. Therefore, competitors' price changes are unlikely to explain my results.
The lack of reaction by competitors could be due to the fact that each state has on average 214 life insurers: 51.3% (39.1%) have P&C affiliates in the permanent (10-year) policy sample, of which on average only 25% experience affiliated P&C losses exceeding 2% of lagged P&C assets in a given four-quarter period. Therefore, it is plausible that the rest of the market does not respond to idiosyncratic shocks to a relatively small part of the market.
article.IV. Internal Financial Transfers and P&C:
Life divisions' financial constraints can tighten if they increase financial transfers to P&C affiliates following P&C losses. Insurers report the net amount they transfer to (or receive from) other entities within the group. However, I cannot perfectly identify the total amount that life divisions transfer to their P&C affiliates. The reason is that life divisions can first make transfers to another entity in the group, often the holding company, which then makes transfers to P&C divisions. In this section, I analyze whether life divisions' transfers to the rest of the group increase with P&C divisions' losses.
Regulations limit the amount an insurance division can transfer to the rest of the group. Most states adopt the NAIC model regulation. The limit is usually 10% of a division's lagged capital for dividends to parents, 2.5% of capital for holdings of investments in affiliates, 10% of capital for guarantees for affiliates, 3% of assets for indirect loans to affiliates, and so on.22
Since insurers report transfers annually, I constructand its instrument using annual data, instead of aggregating over four consecutive quarters as in the regressions on life insurance premiums. I obtain data on life divisions' internal transfers from 1994 to 2014 from NAIC and match them to their P&C affiliates' lagged annual losses.23
In TableVI, Panel A, columns (1) and (2) present OLS results. Columns (3) to (6) present instrumental variable results. In the first stage, the instrument,, is statistically significantly related to reported losses. All of the coefficients onare positive and statistically significant, implying that larger P&C losses are associated with larger transfers out of life divisions. Panel D of TableIA.VIin theInternet Appendixtabulates results for the coefficients on the controls.
Based on column (2), a one-standard-deviation increase in(2.5% of lagged assets) is associated with an increase of 3.0 percentage points (pp) in net transfers made by life divisions (as a percentage of lagged capital), which is 11% of the standard deviation and 29% of the mean. Given that the mean transfer is 10.4% and the median is 6.2%, the average increase of 3.0 pp should on average keep transfers below the threshold requiring regulatory approval. Using the median size of group-level P&C assets, a one-standard-deviation increase in($75.0 million) is associated with a $12.0 million increase in net transfers made by a life division with median-sized capital.25The magnitude is larger using the instrumental variable method. The larger magnitude could again be due to measurement error in reported losses or to weather-related P&C losses presenting better investment opportunities at the P&C divisions than reported P&C losses, as discussed in SectionIII.B.1.
In TableVI, Panel B, I compare the average transfers out of life divisions between subsamples with high versus low affiliated P&C losses. I demean life divisions' transfers, as well as reported and instrumented, by subtracting division and year averages. Instrumented losses are predicted from instrumental variable regression, as in column (3) of Panel A. I classify demeaned(reported or instrumented) as high if it is in the top quintile, and as low otherwise. The comparison indicates that the average transfers made by life divisions in the subsample with high P&C losses are 2.9 pp larger using reported losses or 2.7 pp larger using instrumented losses. Both differences are statistically significant and correspond to around 46% of the median. Figure4, Panel A presents binned scatter plots of transfers out of life divisions against affiliated P&C losses (reported or instrumented). Again, larger P&C losses correspond to larger transfers out of life divisions. TableIA.XIin theInternet Appendixrepeats the regression in Panel A, replacingwith one of two dummy variables,and. The results suggest that the average transfers life divisions make are larger when P&C losses are larger.
The results are consistent with the idea that life divisions become more constrained due to shocks to P&C divisions transmitted through internal capital markets, which can explain the changes in their life policy premiums. The additional near-term financial resources generated by life divisions through price changes can be seen as (partially) offsetting the increased transfers, to avoid being downgraded by the rating agency.26
It is not necessarily the case that the increased transfers made by life divisions ultimately end up at P&C divisions, because the transfers from life divisions can stay at another entity, for example, the holding company. The results in SectionIA.VIof theInternet Appendixindicate that P&C divisions receive more transfers when they incur larger losses, both reported and instrumented. For a median-sized individual P&C division, a one-standard-deviation increase in, $10.4 million, is associated with a $2.4 million increase in transfers it receives. In Figure4, Panel B, the binned scatter plots also confirm a positive relationship between transfers received by P&C divisions and their losses.
Note that P&C losses can tighten life divisions' constraints even if life divisions do not make transfers to P&C divisions. One reason is that AM Best adjusts an insurance division's rating based on the entire group's financial conditions (AM Best (2009)). Such a spillover channel is analogous to that in Gopalan, Nanda, and Seru (2007). Thus, when P&C divisions suffer large losses, life divisions may need to shore up near-term financial resources to prevent a downgrade. Another related reason is that 25% of the life insurer-year observations in my sample receive net transfer inflows from the rest of the group. Large P&C losses can mean fewer contributions from the P&C divisions to the rest of the group, and fewer financial resources at the group level available for life divisions when needed in the future.
The results on internal transfers suggest when P&C divisions incur larger losses (both reported and instrumented), life divisions make more transfers to the rest of the group and P&C divisions receive more transfers from the rest of the group. These results offer corroborating evidence that the changes in life insurance premiums are a result of P&C affiliates' losses, to alleviate the tighter financial constraints. In this regard, this paper relates to studies on how conglomerates can help mitigate financial market frictions, for example, Hadlock and Ryngaert (2001), Erel, Jang, and Weisbach (2015), and Matvos, Seru, and Silva (2018).
article.V. Conclusion:
Frictions in the financial markets can lead firms to be financially constrained. One of the channels through which firms' financial constraints can affect the real economy is through firms' product pricing decisions. In this study, I examine how financial constraints affect firms' pricing decisions in the context of the insurance industry. Using P&C divisions' losses as plausibly exogenous shocks to the financial constraints of life divisions in the same group, I study how life divisions change product pricing in response to these shocks. P&C divisions' losses can increase the shadow value of financial resources for life divisions through internal capital markets but are unlikely to change other aspects of life divisions' business. Because reported P&C losses are known to contain measurement error and can be poor proxies for tightened financial constraints, I also instrument P&C divisions' losses with their exposure to unusual weather damages.
I find that following P&C losses, life insurers change prices in different directions for different policies. First, for permanent life policies, which initially increase insurers' financial resources, premiums decline with affiliated P&C losses. Intuitively, by lowering premiums, life divisions can sell more of these policies and generate more immediate financial resources. In contrast, for 10-year policies, which initially reduce insurers' financial resources, premiums rise with affiliated P&C losses. Intuitively, by increasing premiums, life divisions sell fewer such policies and reduce the outflow of financial resources.
Evidence also suggests that life insurers in more constrained groups change prices to a greater extent. Moreover, following larger P&C losses, life divisions increase financial transfers to the rest of the group. This finding offers corroborating evidence that shocks to P&C divisions are transmitted to life divisions. These two results support the argument that life divisions change prices in response to P&C divisions' losses because P&C losses tighten life divisions' financial constraints.
This study has three main implications, most of which could generalize across industries. First, this paper contributes to the literature by showing that financial constraints can have a causal effect on product pricing decisions. More importantly, this paper shows that the direction in which firms change prices can depend on the products' short-term impact on firms' financial resources. This mechanism can potentially reconcile the mixed findings in the literature. My results also suggest that aggregating prices across industries or across firms within an industry can offset price changes in different directions and in turn mask the effect of financial constraints on product pricing.
Second, this paper contributes to our understanding of firms' internal capital markets. Prior studies focus on whether and how internal capital markets allocate resources across divisions to be spent on investment, R&D, or employment. This paper finds that shocks to some divisions affect other divisions' product pricing, which relates to activities that directly generate financial resources.
Third, this study contributes to the broad literature on financial intermediation, as insurers are significant players in this market. The results suggest that financial frictions can affect insurers' costs and their capacity to provide financial intermediation, even though the risks they insure through life policies are mostly idiosyncratic. Finally, this paper implies that through diversified conglomerates that contain both P&C and life divisions, systemic shocks to one market can affect the prices that households face in the other market.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
article.Appendix B: Changes in Financial Resourc:
Life insurers report the total number of all new term policies issued but not the number of permanent life policies issued. Therefore, I use my results on 10-year term policies to calculate changes in financial resources due to price changes. Life insurers in my sample sell on average 15,138 new term life policies each year. Data do not offer a detailed breakdown of the number of policies sold in terms of policy length, so I assume that all of the term policies that an insurer sells are 10-year policies. According to Friedrich and Saip (2018), the average insured's age at issuance weighted by face amount is 43. Thus, I assume all of the term life policies that a firm sells are 10-year term policies for males at age 40, evenly distributed across the different combinations of health categories and face amounts in my sample.
Column (3) of the lower half of TableIA.Vin theInternet Appendixsuggests that a one-standard-deviation increase in P&C losses (2.5% of assets) leads to a 5.0% change in the life insurance premium. Based on Koijien and Yogo's (2016) demand elasticity estimate of 2.2, this premium change leads to an 11.0% change in the number of policies sold, or 1,665 fewer new policies. Using the average first-year capital impact of 10-year policies for a 40-year-old in my sample (−$539), the 1,665 fewer new policies lead to a reduction in capital outflow of $897,532. In addition, the price increase for the 13,473 (15,138 − 1,665) new policies sold leads to a reduction of $131,362 (13,4735.0%$390/2) in capital outflow, where $390 is the average premium for 40-year-olds (dividing by two because of an assumed commission rate of 50%). In total, the price change leads to a $1,028,892 ($897,532+$131,360) reduction in capital outflow in the first year.
Using the average capital impact in the first two years of these 10-year policies (−$667), the 1,665 fewer new policies lead to a $1,110,555 ($6671,665) reduction in capital outflow. In addition, the price increase for the 13,473 new policies sold leads to a $394,085 (13,4735.0%$3901.5) reduction in capital outflow (assuming the commission is 50% of the premium in the first year). In total, the price change leads to a $1,504,640 ($1,110,555 + $394,085) reduction in capital outflow in the first two years.
Repeating the exercise for changes in cash flows or assets, in the first year the price change leads to a $1,110,555 reduction in outflow because the asset change is the same as the capital change in the first year. Combining the first two years, the price change leads to a reduction of $1,013,465 ($3721,635+13,5034.9%$3901.5) in asset (or cash) outflow, where $372 is the average impact on assets in the first two years of these policies for 40-year-old insureds.
Because a group with P&C divisions has on average 3.7 life divisions, the total effect of the price changes across all life divisions is a $5.6 million reduction in capital outflow and a $3.7 million reduction in asset outflow. I repeat this calculation changing the following assumptions. First, I use alternative demand elasticity, which is two standard errors above or below 2.2. The standard error is 0.8 (2.20.5/1.3, based on Table D.1 in Koijen and Yogo (2016)). Thus, the alternative elasticity is 3.8 and 0.6. Second, I use alternative insured ages of 30 and 50. (TableAIIreports the average premiums and impact on capital and cash flows or assets for each policy in the first two years.) In addition, I use the OLS coefficient, which is 0.51 using the premium as a percentage of the product average as the dependent variable. However, the OLS coefficient is likely biased toward zero as I argue in SectionsIII.B.1andIII.C.4, and thus understating the effect of price changes on insurers' financial resources. Below I report the impact of price changes on capital and cash flow or assets based on these calculations.
